key_insight: Capillary thinning is best treated as a benchmark complex flow, and its output should be organized by an elastic Weissenberg number and a geometry-controlled Deborah number rather than a single apparent relaxation time.
engineering_relevance: This framework helps compare and calibrate constitutive models for viscoelastic polymer solutions across devices, concentrations, and molecular weights in extension-dominated flows.
method: Recast capillary-breakup rheometry results into an elastocapillary Pipkin diagram using Wi_e versus Deborah number.
Use a single-mode mid-filament stress balance to derive universal thinning curves with low-De plateau and finite-extensibility effects.
Apply a scaling collapse with an elastic-onset-referenced Deborah number; use the C2D2 model to capture coil-stretch hysteresis and concentration-dependent drag.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a master-curve collapse across decades in molecular weight and concentration and across multiple devices, with C2D2 matching trends that the classical FENE-P model cannot.
watch_item: The framework depends on estimating unmeasured initial prestretch and geometry-dependent Deborah number, so careful protocol and device characterization are essential.
paper_title: To win, a model must thin: Capillary thinning as a benchmark complex flow for constitutive models of viscoelastic polymer solutions
